title: The Shape of a Thought
note: TIGHTENED cut. One entry per beat; each idea is stated once and the visuals carry the rest. `id` matches the beat key in scenes.py and the audio filename audio/<id>.mp3. Order is the play order of the concatenated narration.

beats.text: Someone closes their eyes and imagines squeezing their left hand. They don't move a muscle. And yet a computer in the room writes the word: left.
No magic — just a few wiggling voltages, and a piece of geometry I want to derive with you, from nothing.
An EEG is a handful of electrodes, each recording its own voltage over time. Here are four of them.
The obvious idea — compare these numbers directly — fails. The amplitude drifts with the gel, the day, the person; the same thought, recorded twice, can look numerically nothing alike.
What's stable is how the channels move together. Imagine the left hand, and a certain set of regions rises and falls in concert. That co-movement is the fingerprint — so let's measure exactly that.
Stack the channels into a matrix X, subtract each channel's mean, and form X times X-transpose, over the number of samples.

That's the covariance matrix. The diagonal is each channel's power; the off-diagonals are how channels co-vary. A whole multi-second recording, compressed into one small object — the shape of the trial.

It is symmetric, and positive definite — because v-transpose Sigma v is just the squared length of X-transpose v, which can never be negative. Symmetric positive definite: S-P-D, forced by what covariance is.
So each trial is one point, in the space of all SPD matrices. Take the two-by-two case: three numbers — a, b, and c — with a and c positive, and a-times-c minus b-squared positive.

Plot every matrix that obeys those rules, and they fill the inside of a cone. Its boundary, where the determinant hits zero, is a wall — a collapsing, degenerate covariance that should feel infinitely far away.
On a curved space the ruler changes from point to point: the trace of P-inverse, U, P-inverse, V. At the identity it's the ordinary flat ruler; toward the wall, every step costs more.
The length of any path is that ruler, integrated along it; the shortest path between two matrices is their geodesic. So what is the shortest route — and why is the straight one a trap?
Two trials. A is diagonal four, one; B is diagonal one, four. Both have determinant four.

Average them entry by entry, and watch the determinant: it climbs to six and a quarter — larger than either one. And that's forced, not bad luck — the determinant of the straight average is always at least the geometric mean of the two. Entry-wise averaging invents variance.

Now travel the cone's own way, along the geodesic. Its determinant holds at exactly four the whole way, because along a geodesic the log-determinant is linear. The straight chord overshoots; the geodesic refuses to inflate — and that gap is the swelling.

Measure that geodesic's length, and it collapses to something clean: the distance is the size of the logarithm of A-to-the-minus-half, B, A-to-the-minus-half — the square root of the sum of squared log-eigenvalues.
The logarithm is the whole point. As an eigenvalue runs toward that collapsing wall, its log runs to minus infinity, and the distance blows up — so the boundary really is unreachable, for free.

And the distance is affine-invariant: transform both matrices by any invertible W, and it doesn't change. Re-referencing the electrodes, rescaling them, the skull mixing sources — all just a W. The ruler is blind to exactly the nuisances that defeated raw voltage.
To average many trials, don't average their entries — take the point that minimizes the total squared geodesic distance to all of them. That's the Riemannian mean: one prototype per class.
Decoding is then almost trivial. A new trial goes to the nearest prototype — minimum distance to mean. No deep network; the geometry did the work.
And if you want the ordinary machine-learning toolbox, flatten the cone at the mean with the log map. There, a linear classifier is doing honest Riemannian geometry — not an approximation.
None of this needed a bigger model — only the right geometry. The notebook runs all of it, end to end, on a real recording. Go open it, and watch the math become a decoder.
